speaker: interviewer
text: May 3, 1995. We're about to start tape number four. Interview with Mrs. Lilly Tykocinski.

speaker: interviewee
text: You said it very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. So when we left off, you were going toward the American soldiers, and it was May 2, 1945. May 2, 1945. So we slept the night through. You know, the night fell in, and we slept the night through in a barn. All of us, maybe seven people, we were hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were told that the Polacks are working in German cities, and they live there, in the towns, that we shouldn't roam around the streets. We should hide. So we were sleeping in a barn. The next morning, we got up. So we heard a noise, so we went out to the road. The American tanks were going. The Germans were throwing the the arms. But we couldn't speak English. They didn't know who we are. We couldn't identify with them. There was no communication. There was no happiness for me, because I didn't know what's going on. And the Germans were quiet. So the American troops went by, and they said that the Russians are coming. So we started running towards the Americans, not to be with the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were afraid of the Russians. We wanted to be liberated by the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you afraid of the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you. See, I had-- I worked-- we had an old-- and older lady. We had a lady, a Czechoslovakian, with us. She was a block over by us. But she was a good person, and a famous person from home. Her-- her husband or brothers-- husband's brother was Smetana. He was a conductor, or a music writer, in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was-- [INAUDIBLE] so she told us-- so we were running towards the Americans, but we didn't run fast enough. So we-- we came through the whole day running to a place where there was a big magazine-- an empty building. So we went in there. The night was done.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had the two Polacks with us. So we kept together. And I says, let's stay over the night. But the Russians came in at night. You know, they took over. And we closed this place. And the Russians start knocking on the door, and they wanted to tear apart the door to see who it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we opened the door. And we were hiding. There was-- this was a magazine from shoes or something, so there was a shelf on top. So we all-- girls-- went upstairs. And the Slovakian woman started speaking to them in Russian, that we are from a concentration camp, liberated. So they wanted to take the-- everybody says, this is my husband. The Polacks-- they wanted to kill the Polacks. And we-- we screamed so much, and we cried so much, that one Russian says, oh, leave them alone. And they walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the next day, a Russian came to me, and he started talking. I'm going to give you shoes. And he bent down. He touched my feet. And I'm going to give you sugar. And I'm going to come to you 12 o'clock. And I says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We were screaming. We were talking. He says he's going to get some more rations. And he went. He was so drunk that he fell in a ditch, and he fell asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it came like 10, 12 o'clock in the morning. We say that it's no way to stay here, because we're going to be-- you know, the Russians, they're wild, wild animals. This was the [INAUDIBLE] Cossacks or whatever. So we-- so intelligent, rational young ones, an educated one, and we told him that we are from camp. And you liberated us. Give us something to go back someplace. So the Polacks took from Germany the horses and wagons, and they were going back to Poland. So he stopped the wagon. He says, take those girls with you, wherever you go. And we were going with those Polacks, with the horse and wagon, back. We didn't know which way we go. But we were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every night, we stopped in some kind of empty house, and we slept. And the Russians were there, and the Russians were there. They didn't let off. No. But in one place, we stopped with an empty house. So we left the Polacks go, and we stayed there. Germans left. It was a German town. The Germans left. They left an old, old dying man there, laying in one room. So we occupied the house. There were some food left. Made ourselves comfortable. And the Russians were coming to check it. And there was one Russian-- he was Polish-- and a Jew. And he was from the National Army, a pilot or something. And he was overwhelmed to hear that we survived. And he says, I'll take care. I'll send you back to Poland. And he gave the orders not to be touched, that nobody should come. Nobody should touch us. He brought us food. And he said, with the first location, he will get a car, and he will bring us to Stettin, to go back to Poland. And we stayed there two or three days. And then we got food. We ate. He gave me a gun, and he says, kill this German guy. Kill him. And I stood there, and I says, I cannot do it. He begged for water. Water, water. I says, you're not going to get water. Nobody killed him. He stayed there. We could not do it. And after a few days, he took us to Stettin with a truck and we boarded a train. The Russians offered us to stay in Stettin, to give us jobs, to organize something. I says, I'm going to see where my brothers-- if my brothers survived. And we went on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get a train going to Poland, with the-- Polish army was going back from someplace. We got on the train, and we went to Lodz-- to Poland. We arrived in Lodz in the middle of the night, with nowhere to go. So while the train stopped in Poznan or some place, at a station, a Jewish man was in the other train. And he says, where are you going, girls? And I says, we're going to Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I stay already in Lodz, and this is my address. Go to our house. There is somebody already there. You can go. He gave me the address. And he said, because he's going already-- he was going, doing business to make a living. And we stayed overnight in the Red Cross building in Lodz. And in the morning, we walked to this address that he gave me. And we came, and there was a beautiful house, and there were two guys and a girl already there. Whoever didn't have anywhere to go. We came in. We took a bath, and they gave us eggs and rolls to eat. And it was the best breakfast we ever had. This with the day of the liberation. May 3, I was liberated. I didn't feel that I'm liberated. We were scared for our lives because of the Russians. The Russians raped the girls. We were the luckiest five, that we were not touched. I was hiding in the cars on the bottom, covered with rags. They shouldn't see me. Everybody-- my girlfriend says, you put your head down if the Russians are coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were lucky that we went through till we got to Poland. And in this house, I left my girlfriends-- the one that lives in Seattle and the one in Israel-- and I took off with another one to go to my hometown. And we came to Zawviercie the next day to look who was here. I didn't find my brothers. I found friends-- a few friends, a few good friends, somebody from the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed in Zawviercie a few months with friends. You know, they had already taken over the house-- a Jewish house that it was once. And we stayed there, and we started to live, to see who was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And being in the house, a man knocks on the door. And I opened the door, and he looks, and he says, I know you. I says, what is he, crazy or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw your picture. I was with your brothers in camp, and they are in Bergen-Belsen. And they are alive. He said that I'm not coming back without you, because I told them if I find anybody, I'll bring you back. I thought in  the beginning that he is nuts. But he told me, you know, what picture, because I did send my brothers a picture. And we stayed in Poland maybe two months, for May, June, July. And there was the first pogrom in Kelz or some place, and I was scared. I says, let's get out. And a friend of mine and myself and this guy, and we took to go to Germany, because we were afraid to stay in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a pogrom. Who was committing the pogroms?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polacks. The Polacks. The Polacks-- for the Jews that came back. It was in Kelz, they were killing a few Jews. And I says, why should I stay here? Nobody's here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went back to the house that we owned, in every room, somebody else lived. They looked out through the windows. They looked at me like I am-- I came from the moon. Oh, you're alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't comfortable there. So I says, let's go back to Germany. Let's go back to look for my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about the pogroms?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard. We heard. Now, in Poland, [INAUDIBLE] free time. You know, we heard that here is going on. Here is something going on. So we heard. There was already a Jewish committee, and a few survivors came back that survived other camps. And everybody was looking for their family. So we picked ourselves up, and we are going to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went through Prague, and then we went through the border, and we came to Germany, to Straubing. And over there, I met already a few people from my hometown, that they settled in Germany. And I decided to stay to look around what's going on and let my brothers know that I'm here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this guy that went with us went to Bergen-Belsen. And I says, I'm staying here. Tell them to come. And slowly by slowly, each and one of them came back to Germany. One of my brothers was liberated not far from Straubing. And I heard he was alive when he came. This was the one that I stayed in the ghetto till the last with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I says, you excuse me that I'm alive. I says, I don't know what to say. Why me? Why am I alive, not the others? And we hugged, and we kissed, and we talked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no explanation, why did I survive, because he was not in Auschwitz. He went to another camp. And then my other brothers came from Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my younger brother, Mendel, said, I can't get used to her. She's grown up. Her voice is different. We left a child here. For five years, he didn't see me. I can't get used to it. So we started to rebuild our life in Germany. And the Joint was then. There were Americans, Jewish-Americans. They gave us a lot of support in Germany. It came the holidays, so they took over the German theater. And he asked, who knows how to sing and conduct services? And we conducted services. We had people singing and praying. And we got to know each other, everybody. And my brothers came and took an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German says, I'll get you from this German an apartment, from this German-- they gave us privileges. The Germans were sitting very, very, very still, very quiet. They didn't say anything. So we lived in Germany for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that your brothers didn't recognize you. You looked so different after the five years. When you first saw them, did you recognize them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they look to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were already grown up, you know. They were already-- one was 18, 20, 22. So they were already like here in the picture. But I was 11, 12. And when I came out, I was 16. So I was already talking mature talk. And they left me as a child, as a baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got to know each other. And then we were looking who we can find, and we found a cousin. She was in a DP-- Displaced Camp. And then we brought her to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: She married my oldest brother in Germany. And you know, my-- one of my brothers got from the Americans a business. He took over a restaurant business from a German, with beer, with the-- with the whole thing, in a small town next to Straubing. And he lived there. He was-- the German says, this was a Nazi. Take it. Take his business and run it. And he-- he did, and he was doing business there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in Germany. We went on vacation. We got to know people. We got to meet friends, to learn how to-- to get back to normal life.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was-- this was the time, from '45 till '52. I stayed. I was going to Bergen-Belsen. And I stayed a while in Bergen-Belsen with my brothers, because my younger brother was in Bergen-Belsen liberated, and he was acting as an actor in the-- in the Bergen-Belsen Troupe. And they went to Paris, you know, to perform, and then they came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was going to Bergen-Belsen and coming back to Germany, until we decided to-- my brother got-- met his wife in Paris. And they came back to Germany. They got married. And then we decided to go to America. We had the papers to go to America. Why did you decide to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't want to stay in Germany. There was no future for us in Germany. There was no future for us in Poland. There was no place to go. So the only place was-- and my older brother went in 1947 to Israel. And I don't know if they were very happy there in the beginning, because they had a baby, with a baby. And when she wrote, she says, take your time. Don't come yet. So I decided maybe we should go to America. We didn't have-- my sister-in-law had an uncle in America. So we applied. The Joint and everybody was giving you visas to the continent. And we applied, and we waited to go to America. So in 1952, February 4, my brother and my sister-in-law, then eight months pregnant, and myself, as a chaperone, we came by plane to America. And how luckily, the plane got-- one engine got very-- was broken in Iceland. And we sit-- sat in Iceland eight hours, till they fixed the plane. I says, God, let me see America. [LAUGHS] Let me New York. And we arrived February 4, after three days flying with a Flying Tiger, a military plane. I was the only young person on the plane. They were all old and sick people. And my sister-in-law was pregnant. And I was the only young person on the plane. And we arrived to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a stopover. It was supposed to be a stopover, because they had a contract to St. Louis. So in the meanwhile, we stayed eight days. My nephew was born, on February 12, Lincoln's birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in the Bellevue Hospital-- in those days, the NYU was once the Bellevue-- because we lived on 32nd Street there, and we didn't know where to go, what to do. So we took her to this hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody said to me, it was the only white child between all the blacks in this hospital. Somebody says, you never know. He might be a president of this country. He was born on Lincoln's day. And I didn't know what they were talking about. I didn't know the American history, the presidents. How was your English at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned, in Germany, a little bit of English. But when I came, I could communicate a little bit. But when the baby was born, I says, I wasn't prepared for baby communication. So the Joint-- the [? highers ?] let us stay in the hotel, because my sister-in-law thought she was in the eighth month, and she was in the ninth month. And then I had to go and buy clothes for the baby. So I went to the store. I says, everything for baby. And I gave them money, and we communicated somehow. Somehow we managed. But we loved this country the day we arrived. I loved the United States then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was free. I was free as a Jew. I lived-- we lived in the hotel a few weeks, on 32nd, off 5th Avenue. And then we rented an apartment in Brooklyn, because friends of ours lived there. And we had, next door, a temple, and the voices came into our room. And Saturday, everybody was in the street on Eastern Parkway. And it was so beautiful. And I says, you can say you're a Jew. You're not afraid. And we survived, and it's so happy. We blessed every day. We kissed. We sweat. But I says, it's so beautiful here. It's so nice. Those days, New York was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you get a job at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. My brother was starting looking for business. He wanted to buy a farm. You know, he came. He had some money, you know, from Germany. He worked in Germany. He made some money. And I went to school, to evening school. And I finished two years' evening school. I graduated with a diploma. And they promised to give me a job, but I got my own job. I went from agency to agency, determined to get a job, not in a factory as a seamstress, but in an office. And I got my first job in a National Credit office on Park Avenue Two. There were no Jews there-- two Jewish girls. But they treated me royally. This was some-- I worked like from February. And everybody was very, very nice to me, and they listened. Oh, a survivor, and oh, when they saw my number, everybody cried. And everybody pitied me. And everybody wanted to look at me like I was from the other planet, that I survived something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like that kind of treatment, or-- I mean, how did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little embarrassing, but liked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked it. Because the people were nice. They make you feel nice. They were older gentlemen. They were reporters. And everybody was so gentle. And they tried not to hurt me. And the boss-- I had a boss lady, an elderly lady. And it was hot then came the summer that year. And it was so hot that I was sweating. So they advised me to take salt pills for sweating. And everybody was very, very nice to me-- no critics, no nothing. They didn't even want to ask too much. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked quite a few months, till I met my husband. I knew my husband already, because we met in Germany once, on vacation. But nothing was serious. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was going out. I had dates. And then my husband proposed, and I just accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seymour Robbins. And he gave me an engagement ring. And I had to show off at work my engagement ring, you know. Oh, everybody was happy for me. And then I said that I'm quitting the job. They were very sad, because for this position that I had, they said they trained so many people, and nobody from the Americans could take over a job like I had. It was not as a typist. It was not as a secretary. I had to-- to maintain the work that comes in and that goes out. So I could work around the whole department, free to bring the work, to take the work from them. So it was a job to remember. I have a good memory. And they says, only somebody from Europe can do that, because they told me, they had a Polish girl on this position. She's the head of the IBM machines now. So they gave me the job, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in 1954 in-- Thanksgiving. November 24th. And I stopped working. And we had a new apartment. And I was happy. I was on my own for the first time. I had my own bed, my own house. [DRINKING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your husband also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was a survivor. He was from a town-- a little town next to Lublin. He was in the partisans. He was a big fighter. And he had a brother and  a sister here. And, you know, we-- I moved to Forest Hills then, with our new apartment. I was extremely happy that-- that I had my peace. I says, I never dreamed if I'm going to have my home, my bed, my bathroom, and food enough to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband was in business. He was in hotel business, real estate. He was already in business when we married. He was working. And then, after three years, I think-- '57-- my son was born, Harvey, my oldest. He was a beautiful baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two years later, two years nine months, I had my other son, Mel. And this is-- this is my family that I raised all the years. My husband was a partisan, and then he organized when the children got a little bigger. And he started organizing the partisan organization. He became the president of the organization. And he sat there talking about the camps, about the fighters, that the Jews did not go to slaughter as sheeps. And he was fighting. And he spoke the first time in Dov Revel, here in the Jewish School. I have papers. For the first time-- that the people heard that something existed like this. And he-- we had meetings. And we had dinners. You know, we had demonstrations. If there was something to be demonstrated, he was the one to be the first one to go. And he was the one to say that we have to teach in school. That we have to write newspapers about it. In the midst of everything, he got sick. You know, when he was-- he was the first one to deport this Braunstein. There was a woman. She was a guard in Majdanek. And my husband liberated Majdanek. He was the first one to liberate Majdanek. And he saw the ovens still burning, as partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he knew about this woman, this SS woman. And people came here. And he was supposed to testify at her trial, to open the trial. And he couldn't anymore. He got sick. He had cancer. He couldn't speak. He couldn't testify anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this-- he started this whole talking about Holocaust, my husband. The Partisan Hymn-- he started singing it every occasion they gathered together. Any party, any gathering to talk about-- they were singing the Partisan Hymn. This-- my husband started this whole business. And he left in the midst of everything. In 1972, he passed on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there came a time that you remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years later, 1975.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your second husband's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Tykocinski. And I'm married, 20 years it will be, the 11th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sons are living where?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger son lives in California, in Laguna Niguel. And my older son lives in Denver, Colorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are they married, either one of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger son is married, and he has a baby. The baby will be five months the 9th of May. And my older son is not married anymore at the present. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to add at this point, that you think maybe you left out?

speaker: interviewee
text: That my son-- my husband has two children. His son, Mark Tykocinski-- he lives in Cleveland. He's a doctor in research, a scientist. And he's very well known. He was just honored in Atlanta, Georgia, last-- before Pesach-- with the highest honor, as a scientist, as the youngest scientist. And the daughter is married. She lives in New York. She's married to a doctor, Dr. Eisenbaum. Joseph Eisenbaum. He's an ophthalmologist. And she has a doctorate from Yale, and they both work together in the office. They have two children. The daughter has two children. One is finishing college this year, and one is finishing high school. And the son in Cleveland has four children-- small ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Holocaust often?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot. I-- I think. I dream. I get nightmares. I cannot see now when I watch the movies. It makes me upset. I cannot watch. The other day, the liberation of Bergen-Belsen, all those camps-- I just could not see it, because I cannot read anything about the Holocaust at night, because I dream. And I'm dreaming that I'm running with my children through this bridge, and then the Germans are on the bridge here. I cannot pass. And this goes on for 50 years. And today is 50 years, May 3, that I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Briefly, on the 50th anniversary of your liberation, what message would you want to give to the world about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: That it should never happen to anybody, what we went through. Even-- any-- any color or any race or any religion should have to suffer what we as Jews suffered. But we'll never give in, no matter what. If Hitler didn't destroy, nobody, will ever destroy us. And we shouldn't forget what happened. We should be on watch for the future. It should not happen. Our children and grandchildren have a-- should have a secure future, because my parents didn't think what's going to happen to us, that we're not going to-- that they lost everybody. I have-- I had survived with four brothers. I'm left with two. My two brothers passed away. So I'm holding on dearly to life, as long as God will give me to live, and hopefully, in health.
